summary: Joel announces the migration of Joel on Software from UserLand Manila to his own company's product, CityDesk, a desktop content management tool. He explains the practical differences between the two approaches: Manila dynamically generated pages from a database, while CityDesk produces static HTML files uploaded via FTP. The migration involved tedious manual copy-and-paste work, which doubled as thorough testing of CityDesk. He emphasizes his commitment to avoiding linkrot by implementing redirects for all old URLs. The post also briefly teases a reader question about 'hit and run management' style.
key_points: Joel migrated his blog from UserLand Manila to CityDesk, his own company's product, practicing what he preaches about eating your own dogfood
CityDesk uses a static HTML generation model (desktop app producing files uploaded via FTP) rather than Manila's dynamic database-driven page rendering
The painful manual migration process served as a rigorous real-world test of CityDesk's capabilities
Joel prioritized URL preservation by implementing redirects to prevent linkrot during the migration
money_quotes: My company's product CityDesk is in beta, and I'm a strong believer in eating your own dogfood.
CityDesk is a desktop application — I'm running it on my own Windows 2000 laptop. It produces plain ol' static HTML files which are then ftp'ed to a generic web server.
Porting Joel on Software to CityDesk involved a lot of manual copying-and-pasting — something I never would have had the patience for if it wasn't for the opportunity to thoroughly test CityDesk.
I'm bending over backwards not to create linkrot — all old links to Joel on Software stories have been replaced with redirects, so they should still work.
Joel on Software has always been free and ad-free, thanks to sponsorship from UserLand and from Fog Creek Software.
key_insight: Using your own product (dogfooding) turns tedious migration work into valuable product testing, aligning business incentives with quality assurance.
